/ PtolemyIIAccessorTutorial Writing your first accessor using Ptolemy II has a number of different steps. Install Ptolemy II See Ptolemy II for how to install Ptolemy II from the svn devel tree. Ptolemy II 10.0 is not up to date , use the svn devel

emy II 10.0 is not up to date , use the svn devel tree instead. The TerraSwarm Accessors repository The accessors are online at https://ptolemy.berkeley.edu/accessors . If Ptolemy II is built from the Ptolemy II svn repository, then a copy of https:/

Ptolemy II svn repository, then a copy of https://ptolemy.berkeley.edu/accessors is checked out in $PTII/org/terraswarm/accessor/accessors/web and when you reload an accessor, the local copy is used. The reason this is done is so as to make it easi

The reason this is done is so as to make it easier to develop accessors that will be eventually added to the https://ptolemy.berkeley.edu/accessors website. If you check out the ptII svn repository via anonymous, read-only access, then the copy of

via anonymous, read-only access, then the copy of https://ptolemy.berkeley.edu/accessors will be checked out anonymously. If you check out the ptII svn repository via read/write access, then the copy of the https://ptolemy.berkeley.edu/accessors will

of the https://ptolemy.berkeley.edu/accessors will be checked out read-write if you have read/write access to that repo. The copy of https://ptolemy.berkeley.edu/accessors is checked out when ./configure is run, when a model that contains an accessor

ure is run, when a model that contains an accessor is loaded or when an accessor is reloaded. So, to get started, either run: cd $PTII . / configure [$[Get Code]] Or open a model that has accessors, such as $ PTII / bin / vergil org / terraswarm / ac

uch as $ PTII / bin / vergil org / terraswarm / accessor / demo / StockTick / StockTick.xml [$[Get Code]] This will create or update $PTII/org/terraswarm/accessor/accessors/web : bash-3.2$ ls $PTII / org / terraswarm / accessor / accessors / web Acce
org / terraswarm / accessor / accessors / web Accessor_1.dtd cameras jsdoc svn.html README.md copyright.txt localization updateIndex audio demo
pdateIndex audio demo net utilities basicFunctions.js devices obsolete vendors basicFunctionsPtDoc.xml doc renderHTML.xsl wiki
renderHTML.xsl wiki browserHost.js image robotics browserHostPtDoc.xml index.html services build.xml index.json signals bash-3.2$ [$[Get Code]] The pr

signals bash-3.2$ [$[Get Code]] The precise contents of the directory may change, but the key thing to note is that there is an index.json file and there are subdirectories. When to create an Accessor Accessors are to encapsulate non-triv

an Accessor Accessors are to encapsulate non-trivial bits of functionality that are suitable for reuse. The same holds for modules, but a module may be host-dependent, whereas an accessor may not. For simple functionality (such as a ramp) just use J

r simple functionality (such as a ramp) just use JavaScript. Note that the Cape Code Host can have both JSAccessor actors (which are wrappers for accessors) and JavaScript actors, which can be used for simple functionality. Overview of Accessors If

or simple functionality. Overview of Accessors If you are not yet familiar with Accessors , see Getting Started . In particular, see Publications And Presentations The quotes below are from the web page : "Accessors are defined in a JavaScript file

age : "Accessors are defined in a JavaScript file that includes a specification of the interface (inputs, outputs, and parameters)" The Accessor files below are provided to us. The WebSocketClient accessor is declared in accessors/web/net/WebSocketCl

essor is declared in accessors/web/net/WebSocketClient.js in the accessors svn repo (which is world readable). JSDoc for WebSocketClient Accessors Library Click on net -> WebSocketClient (this is primarily for the Browser Host , and includes the JSDo

arily for the Browser Host , and includes the JSDoc) The WebSocketServer accessor is declared in accessors/web/net/WebSocketServer.js in the accessors svn repo (which is world readable). JSDoc for WebSocketServer Accessors Library Click on net -> Web

SocketServer Accessors Library Click on net -> WebSocketServer (this is primarily for the Browser Host , and includes the JSDoc) "Any JavaScript file that conforms with the accessor specification defines an accessor class." We will create a composite

nes an accessor class." We will create a composite Accessor that instantiates the WebSocketClient and WebSocketServer accessors. See Composite Accessor below. "The TerraSwarm accessor library provides a collection of example accessors. This library

s a collection of example accessors. This library is maintained via an SVN repository that permits many contributors to add accessors to the library. " We are going to create Node.js module files that are invoked by the pre-existing Accessor declara

t are invoked by the pre-existing Accessor declarations. The good news is that: The Cape Code Host has definitions for webSocketClient and webSocketServer modules that we can use as a template for the Node Host definitions for those modules. WebSocke

Node Host definitions for those modules. WebSocketClient and WebSocketServer Accessor declarations were designed with the ws Node.js interface . The less good news is that modules names in the Java-based Cape Code Host and the Node-based Node Host d

ased Cape Code Host and the Node-based Node Host differ: In Cape Code, Java package names cannot have hyphens, so module names like web-socket-client are converted to webSocketClient . In the Node Package Manager (npm), package names cannot have uppe

kage Manager (npm), package names cannot have upper case letters. We follow the npm-standard because Node is our target audience. Another minor issue is that Java does not support @ signs in package names In Cape Code, the @accessor-modules npm scop

names In Cape Code, the @accessor-modules npm scope is converted to modules and these modules may be found in $PTII/ptolemy/actor/lib/jjs/ modules / . The Big Picture We have Accessor declaration files in the accessors svn repo We have the Cape Code

s in the accessors svn repo We have the Cape Code Host module files that implement the necessary modules for use by the Cape Code Host in Ptolemy II. We will create module definition files that implement the necessary modules for use by the Node Host

ent the necessary modules for use by the Node Host. We will create a Composite Accessor that instantiates the WebSocketClient and WebSocketServer Accessors. Files to Modify We are adding files that define the Node.js module implementation for web-soc

fine the Node.js module implementation for web-socket-client and web-socket-server. Get write access to the accessors svn repo We are implementing modules that will be used by the Accessor declaration, so the directories and files we create are sligh

, so the directories and files we create are slightly different than the Accessor declarations. When in doubt, take a look at the Cape Code Host and $PTII/ptolemy/actor/lib/jjs/modules . Focusing first on the WebSocketClient, the Accessor declaratio

st on the WebSocketClient, the Accessor declaration file at accessors/web/net/WebSocketClient.js contains: var WebSocket = require ( '@accessors-modules/web-socket-client' ) ; [$[Get Code]] so the module we are creating is web-socket-client . As docu

ule we are creating is web-socket-client . As documented in Node.js modules the require() function searches for modules in a particular order. For the Node Accessor host, we currently run node.js in accessors/web/hosts/node/ , which means that requi

accessors/web/hosts/node/ , which means that require() searches in the node_modules/ directory. More specifically, if node.js is run in accessors/web/node/ and require('@accessors-modules/web-socket-client') is invoked, then accessors/web/node/node_m

lient') is invoked, then accessors/web/node/node_modules/@accessors-modules/web-socket-client/package.json is searched for. (''Note that other files such as index.js etc. are searched for after package.json is searched for. See Node.js modules . S

ge.json is searched for. See Node.js modules . So, we will create these two files: accessors/web/hosts/node/node_modules/@accessors-modules/web-socket-client/package.json accessors/web/hosts/node/node_modules/@accessors-modules/web-socket-client/we

de_modules/@accessors-modules/web-socket-client/web-socket-client.js Since there already is a CapeCode module, we can copy it over and modify it: cd accessors / web / hosts / node / mkdir node_modules /@ accessors - web / web - socket - client cp $PT

/@ accessors - web / web - socket - client cp $PTII / ptolemy / actor / lib / jjs / modules / webSocketClient / package. json node_modules /@ accessors - web / web - socket - client cp $PTII / ptolemy / actor / lib / jjs / modules / webSocketClient

y / actor / lib / jjs / modules / webSocketClient / webSocketClient. js node_modules /@ accessors - web / web - socket - client [$[Get Code]] ' Do something similar for the webSocketServer module: mkdir node_modules /@ accessors-web / web-socket-serv

ir node_modules /@ accessors-web / web-socket-server cp $PTII / ptolemy / actor / lib / jjs / modules / webSocketServer / package.json node_modules /@ accessors-web / web-socket-server / cp $PTII / ptolemy / actor / lib / jjs / modules / webSocketSer

olemy / actor / lib / jjs / modules / webSocketServer / webSocketServer.js node_modules /@ accessors-web / web-socket-server / [$[Get Code]] Future work: Create a template module file Create a script that given a module name, creates the module file.

that given a module name, creates the module file. File Modifications Make the following edits to accessors/web/hosts/node/node_modules/@accessors-modules/web-socket-client/package.json : Add yourself as an author: "contributors" : [ { "name" : "Chri

as an author: "contributors" : [ { "name" : "Christopher Brooks" , "email" : "cxh@eecs.berkeley.edu" } , [$[Get Code]] Make the following edits to accessors/web/hosts/node/node_modules/@accessors-modules/web-socket-client/web-socket-client.js : Upda

ules/web-socket-client/web-socket-client.js : Update the @author tag to reflect your authorship. If you are copying the file, give credit to the source file authors * @ author Christopher Brooks , based on Cape Code webSocketClient. js by Hokeun Kim

sed on Cape Code webSocketClient. js by Hokeun Kim and Edward A. Lee [$[Get Code]] The source file was a Nashorn file, which is used to access Java classes, so these two lines get removed: var WebSocketHelper = Java. type ( 'ptolemy.actor.lib.jjs.mod

etHelper = Java. type ( 'ptolemy.actor.lib.jjs.modules.webSocket.WebSocketHelper' ) ; var EventEmitter = require ( 'events' ) . EventEmitter ; [$[Get Code]] FIXME: we need to deal with the EventEmitter Adding the ws module We will be using the ws Nod

r Adding the ws module We will be using the ws Node.js interface , so we need to add update accessors/web/hosts/node/node_modules/webSocketClient/webSocketClient.js . https://github.com/websockets/ws suggests using: var WebSocket = require ( 'ws' ) ;

suggests using: var WebSocket = require ( 'ws' ) ; [$[Get Code]] While in the accessors/web/hosts/node directory, install the ws module: npm install ws [$[Get Code]] This should create accessors/web/hosts/node/node_modules/ws and other directories. W

osts/node/node_modules/ws and other directories. We will check the modules in to the svn repo after everything is working. supportedReceiveTypes() In the Cape Code Host , the webSocketClient.js file has this method: //////////////////////////////////

as this method: /////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////// //// supportedReceiveTypes /** Return an array of the types supported by the current host for * receiveType arguments. */ exports. supportedReceiveTypes

Type arguments. */ exports. supportedReceiveTypes = function ( ) { return WebSocketHelper. supportedReceiveTypes ( ) ; } ; [$[Get Code]] The above Cape Code Host code will not work for us because it uses the Nashorn Java var WebSocketHelper . Instea

ses the Nashorn Java var WebSocketHelper . Instead, we will create a method that returns an array. To get values returned by the Cape Code supportedReceiveTypes() method, create a small .java file, compile it and run it: bash-3.2$ bash- 3.2 $ cat T

compile it and run it: bash-3.2$ bash- 3.2 $ cat Test.java import ptolemy.actor.lib.jjs.modules.webSocket.WebSocketHelper; import java.util.Arrays; public class Test { public static void main ( String args [ ] ) { System.out.println ( Arrays.toString

args [ ] ) { System.out.println ( Arrays.toString ( WebSocketHelper.supportedReceiveTypes ( ) ) ) ; } } bash-3.2$ javac -classpath $PTII :. Test.java bash-3.2$ $PTII / bin / ptinvoke Test [ application / json, text / plain, JPG, jpg, bmp, BMP, gif,

on / json, text / plain, JPG, jpg, bmp, BMP, gif, GIF, WBMP, png, PNG, jpeg, wbmp, JPEG ] bash-3.2$ [$[Get Code]] Thus, method in the Node Host file accessors/web/hosts/node/node_modules/webSocketClient/webSocketClient.js should look like: //////////

nt/webSocketClient.js should look like: /////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////// //// supportedReceiveTypes /** Return an array of the types supported by the current host for * receiveType arguments. */ exports

rent host for * receiveType arguments. */ exports. supportedReceiveTypes = function ( ) { // These values are based on what Cape Code returns in // ptolemy.actor.lib.jjs.modules.webSocket.WebSocketHelper.supportedReceiveTypes(). // Not sure about th

lper.supportedReceiveTypes(). // Not sure about the validity of 'JPG' and subsequent args. return [ 'application/json' , 'text/plain' , 'JPG' , 'jpg' , 'bmp' , 'BMP' , 'gif' , 'GIF' , 'WBMP' , 'png' , 'PNG' , 'jpeg' , 'wbmp' , 'JPEG' ] ; } ; [$[Get C

'PNG' , 'jpeg' , 'wbmp' , 'JPEG' ] ; } ; [$[Get Code]] supportedSendTypes() If we update Test.java to print the value returned by the Cape Code Host implementation of supportedSendTypes() , then we will see that it has the same value, so the method

ill see that it has the same value, so the method in the Node Host file is very similar: /////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////// //// supportedSendTypes /** Return an array of the types supported by the current

urn an array of the types supported by the current host for * sendType arguments. */ exports. supportedSendTypes = function ( ) { // These values are based on what Cape Code returns in // ptolemy.actor.lib.jjs.modules.webSocket.WebSocketHelper.suppo

or.lib.jjs.modules.webSocket.WebSocketHelper.supportedSendTypes(). // FIXME: Not sure about the validity of 'JPG' and subsequent args. return [ 'application/json' , 'text/plain' , 'JPG' , 'jpg' , 'bmp' , 'BMP' , 'gif' , 'GIF' , 'WBMP' , 'png' , 'PNG'

' , 'BMP' , 'gif' , 'GIF' , 'WBMP' , 'png' , 'PNG' , 'jpeg' , 'wbmp' , 'JPEG' ] ; } ; [$[Get Code]] util.inherits() It is good to sanity check our work by parsing the file. Jumping past the Client constructor, there is a line that needs to be commen

nstructor, there is a line that needs to be commented out so that the Node Host can parse this file. The Cape Code Host includes the following line after the Client constructor: util. inherits ( exports. Client , EventEmitter ) ; [$[Get Code]] Howeve

ts. Client , EventEmitter ) ; [$[Get Code]] However, because we have no util module in the Node Host, when we invoke node on our file, we get an error: bash-3.2$ node webSocketClient / webSocketClient.js / Users / cxh / ptII / org / terraswarm / acce

.js / Users / cxh / ptII / org / terraswarm / accessor / accessors / web / { Main.HowToCreateANodeModule $HOSTS_NODE } / webSocketClient / webSocketClient.js: 175 util.inherits ( exports.Client, EventEmitter ) ; ^ ReferenceError: util is not defined

Emitter ) ; ^ ReferenceError: util is not defined at Object. < anonymous > ( / Users / cxh / ptII / org / terraswarm / accessor / accessors / web / { Main.HowToCreateANodeModule $HOSTS_NODE } / webSocketClient / webSocketClient.js: 175 : 1 ) at Modul

ketClient / webSocketClient.js: 175 : 1 ) at Module._compile ( module.js: 413 : 34 ) at Object.Module._extensions..js ( module.js: 422 : 10 ) at Module.load ( module.js: 357 : 32 ) at Function.Module._load ( module.js: 314 : 12 ) at Function.Module.r

_load ( module.js: 314 : 12 ) at Function.Module.runMain ( module.js: 447 : 10 ) at startup ( node.js: 141 : 18 ) at node.js: 933 : 3 bash-3.2$ [$[Get Code]] FIXME: Not sure what to do here. Do we define a util module and an inherits() function? Sol

fine a util module and an inherits() function? Solution var util = require('util'); In the short term, we comment it out // FIXME: Not sure what to do with util.inherits. // See {Main.HowToCreateANodeModule$ACCESSORS_HOME}/wiki/Main/HowToCreateANodeA

Module$ACCESSORS_HOME}/wiki/Main/HowToCreateANodeAccessor#utilInherits //util.inherits(exports.Client, EventEmitter); [$[Get Code]] Does it parse? As a quick test, we run node on the module file to see if it parses: bash-3.2$ pwd / Users / cxh / ptII

e if it parses: bash-3.2$ pwd / Users / cxh / ptII / org / terraswarm / accessor / accessors / web / { Main.HowToCreateANodeModule $HOSTS_NODE } bash-3.2$ node node_modules / webSocketClient / webSocketClient.js bash-3.2$ [$[Get Code]] The above was

etClient.js bash-3.2$ [$[Get Code]] The above was successful, there were no parse errors. Composite Accessor test Next up, we create a composite Accessor that contains a WebSocketClient and try to run it. Create accessors/web/hosts/node/test/testWebS

it. Create accessors/web/hosts/node/test/testWebSocketClient.js that instantiates our composite: var nodeHost = require ( './nodeHost.js); var a = this.instantiate(' TestWebSocketClient ', ' net / test / TestWebSocketClient '); [$[Get Code]] Create

st / TestWebSocketClient '); [$[Get Code]] Create accessors/web/net/test/TestWebSocketClient.js , which defines the composite: exports. setup = function ( ) { var client = this . instantiate ( 'WebSocketClient' , 'net/WebSocketClient' ) ; } ; [$[Get

ketClient' , 'net/WebSocketClient' ) ; } ; [$[Get Code]] Invoke the Node host on testWebSocketClient.js : bash-3.2$ pwd / Users / cxh / ptII / org / terraswarm / accessor / accessors / web / { Main.HowToCreateANodeModule $HOSTS_NODE } bash-3.2$ node

wToCreateANodeModule $HOSTS_NODE } bash-3.2$ node nodeHostInvoke -timeout 1000 net / test / testWebSocketClient.js Reading accessor at: / Users / cxh / ptII / org / terraswarm / accessor / accessors / web / net / test / testWebSocketClient.js Reading

web / net / test / testWebSocketClient.js Reading accessor at: / Users / cxh / ptII / org / terraswarm / accessor / accessors / web / net / WebSocketClient.js Instantiated accessor testWebSocketClient.js with class net / test / testWebSocketClient.j

t.js with class net / test / testWebSocketClient.js bash-3.2$ [$[Get Code]] Success! We can instantiate a WebSocket accessor using our module. However, it does not actually do anything. Client constructor. Next up in the Cape Code implementation is

uctor. Next up in the Cape Code implementation is the declaration of a Client constructor: /////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////// //// Client /** Construct an instance of a socket client that can send or recei

instance of a socket client that can send or receive messages * to a server at the specified host and port. * The returned object subclasses EventEmitter. * You can register handlers for events 'open' , 'message' , 'close' , or 'error' . * The event

, 'message' , 'close' , or 'error' . * The event 'open' will be emitted when the socket has been successfully opened. * The event 'message' will be emitted with the body of the message as an * argument when an incoming message arrives on the socket.

nt when an incoming message arrives on the socket. * You can invoke the send ( ) function to send data to the server. * * The type of data sent and received can be specified with the 'sendType' * and 'receiveType' options. * In principle, any MIME ty

'receiveType' options. * In principle, any MIME type can be specified, but the host may support only * a subset of MIME types. The client and the server have to agree on the type , * or the data will not get through correctly. * * The default type f

not get through correctly. * * The default type for both sending and receiving * is 'application/json' . The types supported by this implementation * include at least: * * __application / json__: The this.send ( ) function uses JSON.stringify ( ) an

this.send ( ) function uses JSON.stringify ( ) and sends the * result with a UTF- 8 encoding. An incoming byte stream will be parsed as JSON, * and if the parsing fails, will be provided as a string interpretation of the byte * stream. * * __text /

interpretation of the byte * stream. * * __text / \ * __: Any text type is sent as a string encoded in UTF- 8 . * * __image / x__: Where __x__ is one of __json__, __png__, __gif__, * and more ( FIXME: which , exactly? ) . * In this case , the data pa

which , exactly? ) . * In this case , the data passed to this.send ( ) is assumed to be an image, as encoded * on the host, and the image will be encoded as a byte stream in the specified * format before sending. A received byte stream will be deco

fore sending. A received byte stream will be decoded as an image, * if possible. FIXME: What happens if decoding fails? * * The event 'close' will be emitted when the socket is closed, and 'error' if an * an error occurs ( with an error message as a

an * an error occurs ( with an error message as an argument ) . * For example, * < pre > * var WebSocket = require ( 'webSocketClient' ) ; * var client = new WebSocket.Client ( { 'host' : 'localhost' , 'port' : 8080 } ) ; * client.send ( { 'foo' : '

, 'port' : 8080 } ) ; * client.send ( { 'foo' : 'bar' } ) ; * client.on ( 'message' , function ( message ) { * console.log ( 'Received from web socket: ' + message ) ; * } ) ; * client.open ( ) ; * </ pre > * * The above code may send a message even

/ pre > * * The above code may send a message even before the socket is opened. This module * implementation will queue that message to be sent later when the socket is opened. * * The options argument is a JSON object that can contain the following

t is a JSON object that can contain the following properties: * * host: The IP address or host name for the host. Defaults to 'localhost' . * * port: The port on which the host is listening. Defaults to 80 . * * receiveType: The MIME type for incomin

to 80 . * * receiveType: The MIME type for incoming messages, which defaults to 'application/json' . * * sendType: The MIME type for outgoing messages, which defaults to 'application/json' . * * connectTimeout: The time to wait before giving up on a

ctTimeout: The time to wait before giving up on a connection, in milliseconds * ( defaults to 1000 ) . * * numberOfRetries: The number of times to retry connecting. Defaults to 10 . * * timeBetweenRetries: The time between retries, in milliseconds. D

ries: The time between retries, in milliseconds. Defaults to 500 . * * discardMessagesBeforeOpen: If true , discard messages before the socket is open. Defaults to false. * * throttleFactor: The number milliseconds to stall for each item that is queu

r milliseconds to stall for each item that is queued waiting to be sent. Defaults to 0 . * * @ param options The options. */ exports.Client = function ( options ) { options = options || { } ; this.port = options.port || 80 ; this.host = options.host

t = options.port || 80 ; this.host = options.host || 'localhost' ; this.sslTls = options.sslTls || false ; this.receiveType = options.receiveType || 'application/json' ; this.sendType = options.sendType || 'application/json' ; this.connectTimeout = o

pe || 'application/json' ; this.connectTimeout = options.connectTimeout || 1000 ; this.numberOfRetries = options.numberOfRetries || 10 ; this.timeBetweenRetries = options.timeBetweenRetries || 500 ; this.trustAll = options.trustAll || false ; this.tr

his.trustAll = options.trustAll || false ; this.trustedCACertPath = options.trustedCACertPath || '' ; this.discardMessagesBeforeOpen = options.discardMessagesBeforeOpen || false ; this.throttleFactor = options.throttleFactor || 0 ; this.helper = WebS

= options.throttleFactor || 0 ; this.helper = WebSocketHelper.createClientSocket ( this, this.host, this.sslTls, this.port, this.receiveType, this.sendType, this.connectTimeout, this.numberOfRetries, this.timeBetweenRetries, this.trustAll, this.trust

this.timeBetweenRetries, this.trustAll, this.trustedCACertPath, this.discardMessagesBeforeOpen, this.throttleFactor ) ; } ; [$[Get Code]] The above needs to be updated to be a call to the appropriate ws functions. See the ws WebSocket documentation f

ws functions. See the ws WebSocket documentation for how to create a WebSocket, which is the closest to the Cape Code WebSocketHelper createClientSocket(). The documentation for ws WebSocket is pathetic . The options are not documented. It looks lik

ic . The options are not documented. It looks like Cape Code's WebSocketHelper is based on Vert.x, not on the ws Node module. See Also Writing Node bindings for C/C++ Libraries Node Package Manager (npm) WebSocket Implementation Nodes for Node Host

npm) WebSocket Implementation Nodes for Node Host Create a copy of an accessor One way to learn about accessors is to copy something that works and use it. In this example, we will create a copy of the StockTick accessor Go to $PTII/org/terraswarm/a

he StockTick accessor Go to $PTII/org/terraswarm/accessor/accessors/web mkdir myAccessor [$[Get Code]] Copy these files: cp services / StockTick.js myAccessor / MyAccessor.js cp services / StockTickIcon.xml myAccessor / MyAccessorIcon.xml cp services

on.xml myAccessor / MyAccessorIcon.xml cp services / index.json myAccessor / [$[Get Code]] services/StockTick.js defines the accessor, we create a copy. Note that the directory name ( myAccessor ) starts with a lower case letter, but the .js file ( M

rts with a lower case letter, but the .js file ( MyAccessor.js ) starts with an upper case letter. This is the coding standard. services/StockTickIcon.xml defines an icon for the accessor. services/index.json lists the accessors in the directory tha

ndex.json lists the accessors in the directory that are available for import. Edit myAccessor/MyAccessor.js and change StockTick to MyAccessor . Edit myAccessor/index.json so that it refers to MyAccessor.js@@ [ "MyAccessor.js" , ] [$[Get Code]] Impor

sor.js@@ [ "MyAccessor.js" , ] [$[Get Code]] Import the New Accessor Start up Ptolemy and attempt to import the accessor File -> New -> Graph Editor File -> Import -> Import Accessor Click on the "Browse" button and go to $PTII/org/terraswarm/accesso

wse" button and go to $PTII/org/terraswarm/accessor/accessors/web/myAccessor and click on "Open". Note that none of the files are highlighted or selectable. This is because we are selecting the directory myAccessors . In the Import Accessor dialog,

ctory myAccessors . In the Import Accessor dialog, the accessor combobox will list "MyAccessor.js" Click OK. The accessor will appear. Use the New Accessor Note that the name has changed, but the functionality is the same as StockTick accessor. Yo

ctionality is the same as StockTick accessor. You could open up $PTII/org/terraswarm/accessor/demo/StockTick/StockTick.xml , import MyAccessor, set the parameters of MyAccessor to the same values as those of the original StockTick accessor, move th

those of the original StockTick accessor, move the connections from the StockTick accessor to the new MyAccessor and run it. Change the Functionality. Edit org/terraswarm/accessor/accessors/web/myAccessor/MyAccessor.js to change the functionality Do

essor/MyAccessor.js to change the functionality Double Click on the MyAccessor Icon and hit Reload to get the new functionality Modules Some Accessors require JavaScript modules, which, in Ptolemy, are found in $PTII/ptolemy/actor/lib/jjs/modules .

e found in $PTII/ptolemy/actor/lib/jjs/modules . For example, $PTII/org/terraswarm/accessor/accessors/web/net/Discovery.js contains: var discovery = require ( 'discovery' ) ; [$[Get Code]] If you did the above steps and created $PTII/org/terraswarm/

the above steps and created $PTII/org/terraswarm/accessor/accessors/web/myDiscovery/MyDiscovery.js , you could replace var discovery = require ( 'discovery' ) ; [$[Get Code]] with var discovery = require ( 'myDiscovery' ) ; [$[Get Code]] and then cr

uire ( 'myDiscovery' ) ; [$[Get Code]] and then create a copy of $PTII/ptolemy/actor/lib/jjs/modules/discovery : cd $PTII / ptolemy / actor / lib / jjs / modules mkdir myDiscovery cp discovery / discovery.js myDiscovery / myDiscovery.js cp discovery
